citation.proposition: A hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court is the seminal California Supreme Court case establishing the 'Privette doctrine,' which holds that a hirer of an independent contractor is not liable for injuries to the contractor's employees.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This is a foundational case in California tort law.

citation.text: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citation.proposition: Granting summary judgment to a hirer where the subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.reasoning: A search of federal reporters does not reveal a case by this name in the Central District of California at this citation. It appears to be a hallucinated citation.
citations.confidence_reasoning: Standard legal databases do not contain this specific citation.

citation.text: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)
citation.case_name: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
citation.proposition: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.reasoning: The citation 887 F.2d 1204 corresponds to a different case (United States v. $277,000.00 U.S. Currency). There is no Ninth Circuit case by this name at this citation.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation is demonstrably linked to a completely different subject matter.

citation.text: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)
citation.case_name: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
citation.proposition: Compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of the exercise of due care.

citations.reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) actually holds that a hirer is not liable for injuries to an independent contractor's employee even if the hirer failed to comply with safety regulations, unless the hirer affirmatively contributed to the injury.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This is a well-known California Supreme Court decision that limits the 'retained control' exception.

citation.text: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp., 198 Cal.App.4th 223 (2011)
citation.case_name: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp.
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that safety compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability.

citations.reasoning: There is no record of a case titled 'Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp.' in the California Appellate Reports at this citation.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The case name and citation do not appear in legal databases.

citation.text: Blackwell v. Sunrise Contractors, Inc., 45 Cal.App.4th 1012 (1996)
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that safety compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability.

citations.reasoning: 45 Cal.App.4th 1012 corresponds to a different case (People v. $47,050). The case name 'Blackwell v. Sunrise Contractors' does not appear at this citation.

citation.text: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC, 387 S.W.3d 154 (Tex. App. 2012)
citation.case_name: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that safety compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability.

citations.reasoning: 387 S.W.3d 154 corresponds to a Texas case (In re State Farm), not 'Dixon v. Lone Star Structural'.

citation.text: Okafor v. Brightline Builders, Inc., 291 So.3d 614 (Fla. Dist. Ct. App. 2019)
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that safety compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability.

citations.reasoning: 291 So.3d 614 is a Florida case (State v. J.A.), not 'Okafor v. Brightline Builders'.

citation.text: Nguyen v. Allied Pacific Construction Co., 112 Cal.App.4th 845 (2003)
citation.case_name: Nguyen v. Allied Pacific Construction Co.
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that safety compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability.

citations.reasoning: 112 Cal.App.4th 845 corresponds to a different case (In re Marriage of Facter).

citation.text: Reeves v. Summit Engineering Group, 78 Cal.App.4th 531 (2000)
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that safety compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability.

citations.reasoning: 78 Cal.App.4th 531 corresponds to a different case (People v. $20,000 U.S. Currency).

citation.text: Rivera v. Cornerstone Grading & Paving, Inc., L.A. Superior Court Case No. BC-2019-33021 (unpublished minute order, Feb. 3, 2020)
citation.case_name: Rivera v. Cornerstone Grading & Paving, Inc.
citation.citation: L.A. Superior Court Case No. BC-2019-33021
citation.proposition: Denying summary judgment where subcontractor retained control over safety equipment.

citations.reasoning: Trial court case numbers are generally not searchable in public legal databases in the same manner as appellate opinions, and the citation format is non-standard for a citable precedent.
citations.confidence_reasoning: Trial court orders are not binding precedent and are rarely cited in legal briefs unless they are part of the record on appeal.

facts.claim: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
facts.claim_source_doc: Paragraph 3 of the Statement of Undisputed Material Facts
facts.contradiction: The incident occurred on March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date of the incident is a verifiable objective fact stated in the official police report and confirmed by the medical records.

facts.claim: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
facts.claim_source_doc: Paragraph 4 of the Statement of Undisputed Material Facts
facts.contradiction: Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
facts.source_quote: Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The police report explicitly documents the site supervisor's confirmation that the required PPE was being worn.

facts.claim: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date is explicitly stated in the official police report and medical records.
